lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market wheat-growing or wheat-seed operations that repeatedly provide externally contracted, customer-dedicated production or managed growing services.
lens.excluded_activities: Ordinary farms selling owned wheat into commodity channels without an outsourced-service relationship, hobby and lifestyle farms, captive growing units, passive landowners, shells, financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, and custom planting or harvesting contractors classified elsewhere.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Potentially eligible firms earn repeat revenue through production contracts, managed growing, or customer-specific seed and grain arrangements; ordinary merchant grain sales at cash or futures-linked prices are not by themselves outsourced services.

a.bridge: BLS and USDA measure agricultural work and precision-technology adoption rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure in 111140. BLS reports that agricultural workers are concentrated in physical farm labor, while USDA reports automated guidance on well over half of winter-wheat acreage but yield maps, soil maps, and variable-rate technology on only 5% to 25%. I translate remaining exposure to crop scouting, input decisions, machinery guidance, recordkeeping, marketing, compliance, and equipment diagnostics. The employee data omit much owner labor, and wheat is more mechanized than labor-intensive specialty crops, so support-activity staffing may overstate manual-labor exposure.
a.rationale: AI can improve disease and pest recognition, weather and yield analysis, input prescriptions, machinery guidance, maintenance, records, marketing, and compliance. Field operations, equipment repair, grain handling, agronomic judgment, and weather-responsive execution remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A wheat-specific wage and owner-labor task study by farm size, combined with ARMS data on current guidance, variable-rate, scouting, recordkeeping, and autonomous-equipment adoption, would materially change exposure.
caveats: No compensation-to-receipts input is available, so this primitive cannot support a complete labor-opportunity calculation.
OEWS poorly captures self-employed producers and unpaid family labor.
Technology adoption and task mix differ greatly by acreage and region.

rho.rationale: Automated guidance is already widespread on winter-wheat acreage, providing an implementation base for AI scouting, agronomic decision support, documentation, and equipment analytics. Broadband gaps, small-farm economics, seasonal workflows, interoperability, data ownership, weather variability, machinery replacement cycles, and trust in agronomic recommendations limit realized adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year panel data from USDA ARMS linking specific AI and automation tools to labor hours, input use, yield, error rates, farm size, connectivity, and continued use on wheat farms would materially move the range.
caveats: Precision guidance adoption is not equivalent to AI substitution.
The smallest farms have materially lower technology adoption.

e.rationale: No defensible LMM firm-count denominator is available, and ordinary wheat farms sell owned commodities rather than outsourced services. Without a farm-level population and production-contract data, the share meeting the recurring external-service lens cannot be estimated honestly.
e.change_evidence: A verified wheat-farm roster with normalized EBITDA, production-contract and managed-growing revenue, customer concentration, land tenure, operating status, and captive relationships would permit an eligibility estimate.
caveats: The assignment declares no defensible LMM firm count and prohibits replacing it.
USDA farm measures are not directly comparable with consolidated-firm EBITDA.
Most wheat revenue is commodity sale rather than service revenue.

s5.bridge: USDA reports that 38% of US farm producers were age 65 or older in 2022 and the average age was 58.1; it does not measure qualifying control transfers for eligible LMM wheat-service firms. I discount for multiple producers per farm, family succession, land transfers without an operating-company transfer, continued operation, leasing, partial asset sales, closures, and farms outside the lens. High producer age raises the proxy, while family and land-based succession make it overstate external qualifying transfers.
s5.rationale: Aging operators create substantial succession activity, but much transfers within families, through leases, or as land and equipment rather than a qualifying transferable operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: USDA TOTAL and ARMS succession data cross-tabulated for commercial wheat farms, plus transaction records separating operating-company control from land, lease, and family transfers, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Producer counts are not firm counts and farms can have multiple producers.
The data cover all farms, not wheat farms or the LMM band.

q.rationale: A fixed production contract may initially let the operator retain labor and input savings, but customer bargaining, contract resets, landlord rent, machinery and seed pricing, crop-share arrangements, and commodity competition absorb gains. Merchant wheat prices are globally determined, so savings may be retained temporarily yet overwhelmed by price and yield volatility.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-farm production contracts and renewals showing cost-plus terms, input sharing, crop shares, price formulas, landlord participation, and margin persistence after documented efficiency gains would materially change retention.
caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit retention.
Commodity price, weather, yield, insurance, and government payments can dominate farm margins.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real crop-production output to grow 1.4% annually from 2024 to 2034, which would imply about 1.07 over five years for all crops. USDA, however, reports a long-term downward trend in US wheat plantings and production, partly offset by rising yields, and a declining US share of global wheat exports. I lower the wheat base below the broad crop proxy. Weather, crop rotation, prices, and exports can make the realized five-year quantity substantially higher or lower.
d5.rationale: Food demand and yield improvement support continuing wheat output, while competition from corn and soybeans, declining acreage, export competition, drought, and volatile prices constrain US production quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Current USDA ten-year wheat projections by planted acreage, harvested acreage, yield, class, domestic use, and exports, with a constant-quality production index, would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The BLS series covers all crops.
Production quantity is not the same as service demand for the narrow eligible lens.

o.rationale: Wheat demand still requires accountable operators to control land access, crop plans, seed, field equipment, agronomy, chemical application, harvest, storage, insurance, and compliance. AI and autonomous machinery can reduce labor but cannot replace biological production, weather-risk decisions, property control, or operating accountability.
o.change_evidence: Commercial-farm evidence on fully autonomous field operations, managed-farming platforms, and customer self-production, including who holds agronomic, property, chemical, and crop-loss liability, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The accountable operator could consolidate into a larger farm manager or equipment-enabled platform.
Automation may reduce onsite staffing without eliminating the operator.

narrative.executive_view: Wheat farming has real precision-technology potential but fits the recurring outsourced-service lens poorly. Most farms sell owned grain, both labor and firm-count dataset inputs are missing, and eligible contract-growing operations cannot be enumerated.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve scouting, disease recognition, weather and yield decisions, variable-rate inputs, machinery guidance, maintenance, records, marketing, and compliance. Field execution, repairs, land control, agronomy, harvest timing, and weather risk remain operator responsibilities.
narrative.value_capture: Global commodity pricing may temporarily preserve cost savings, while production-contract renewals, land rents, suppliers, and buyer bargaining share gains. Weather and crop prices can overwhelm the AI contribution.
narrative.firm_availability: The eligible LMM population is unknown. Producer aging suggests succession, but farm transitions often occur through family transfer, land sale, or lease rather than sale of a recurring-service operating company.
narrative.demand_durability: Wheat remains a staple, but US acreage and production have trended down as other crops compete for land and export share declines. Yield gains partly offset acreage pressure.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Missing dataset inputs, lens mismatch, owner labor outside OEWS, weather, commodity prices, crop insurance, land tenure, machinery cycles, broadband, and regional agronomy make the record highly uncertain.
narrative.principal_driver: Existing precision-guidance adoption creates a practical base for further AI-assisted wheat operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most wheat farms sell commodities rather than outsourced services, and the target firm population cannot be measured.

sources.title: NAICS definition: Wheat Farming
sources.supports: Defines 111140 as establishments growing wheat and/or producing wheat seeds.

sources.title: Agricultural Workers
sources.supports: Describes agricultural work and reports that crop production employs a majority of crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers.

sources.title: Precision Agriculture in the Digital Era
sources.supports: Reports automated guidance on well over half of winter-wheat acreage, while several mapping and variable-rate technologies covered only 5% to 25% of winter-wheat acreage.

sources.title: AI-Powered Wheat FHB and FDK Assessment Tool Development
sources.supports: Describes a USDA project to deploy AI smartphone-image tools for wheat disease and damaged-kernel assessment.

sources.title: Most Row Crop Acreage Managed Using Auto-steer and Guidance Systems
sources.supports: Documents much lower guidance-system adoption among the smallest winter-wheat farms and identifies connectivity as a requirement.

sources.title: Wheat Sector at a Glance
sources.supports: Reports long-term declines in US wheat plantings and production, partly offset by yields, and a declining US share of world wheat exports.

sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: Farm Producers
sources.supports: Reports 38% of US producers age 65 or older and an average producer age of 58.1 in 2022.

sources.title: Farmland Ownership and Tenure
sources.supports: Describes farm ownership, rental, and succession dynamics and notes that grain-production areas commonly have more than half of farmland rented.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024–34
sources.supports: Projects crop-production real output from $175.8 billion in 2024 to $201.8 billion in 2034, a 1.4% annual compound rate.
